data.body:
Goal:
Close the K-36 follow-up gap: today the quorum policy file
(`recipients.signed.toml`) is itself NOT quorum-gated. A compromised
maintainer with merge access could rewrite the operators list (or drop
the file entirely) and then add a recipient single-handedly. The policy
must be as protected as the bundle.

Verification:
1. After landing, modifying `recipients.signed.toml` requires a separate
   set of quorum signatures (M-of-N from the OLD operators list)
   covering the canonical bytes of the new policy.
2. Pre-modification policy hash is stored alongside the bundle's
   BundleSHA256 in the manifest (or in a sibling `policy.hash` file
   committed under the same flock), so a stale policy can be detected.
3. Test: removing an operator from the policy without quorum signatures
   is rejected at `recipient add` time with a clear message; the same
   action with a valid quorum-signed update succeeds.
4. SECURITY.md threat model "compromised maintainer rewrites policy"
   row gains a mitigation pointer to this knot.
5. Documentation in `docs/recipient-quorum.md` updated to cover the
   policy-update protocol (separate from the recipient-add protocol).

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-50; follow-up to K-36 (thimble-f550).
- Files: internal/quorum/policy.go, internal/store/quorum_gate.go (or new policy_gate.go), docs/recipient-quorum.md, manifest schema (optional new field).
- Backwards compat: when no `policy.hash` is present yet (first-time enrolment), allow a single `--bootstrap-policy` flag, mirroring K-36's bootstrap path. Once enrolled, every change requires quorum.
- Notes: Don't conflate this with the bundle SHA. Policy hash and bundle SHA are independent invariants and can be stored adjacent in the same flocked manifest update.
data.title: Gate recipients.signed.toml under quorum
